steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis, hepatic metastatic disease complication, gallstone-related pancreatitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the elevated lipase (221 IU/L, >3x ULN) and nausea/vomiting are driven by biliary pathology (gallstones, choledocholithiasis, cholecystitis) versus another etiology, and whether the newly identified hepatic lesions and calcified gallstones on CT chest are associated with biliary ductal dilation or acute gallbladder inflammation
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or sonographic Murphy's sign suggesting acute cholecystitis; alternatively, common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as the trigger for pancreatitis; or characterization of the hepatic lesions seen on CT chest
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of elevated lipase, nausea/vomiting, back pain, and CT-identified calcified gallstones makes biliary-origin pancreatitis or acute cholecystitis a leading concern, and right upper quadrant ultrasound is the first-line modality to evaluate the gallbladder and biliary tree in this setting.
grounding: History: nausea/vomitting subsequently. Later that evening, develop lower back pain
Prior imaging CT Chest: calcified gallstones within the gallbladder
Prior imaging CT Chest: numerous ill-defined hypodense hepatic lesions which measure up to approximately 2.5-cm... concerning for metastatic disease
History: stage III colon CA... recently started FOLFOX last ___ finished on ___
History: constipated, lethargy through the weekend
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a lipase more than three times the upper limit of normal alongside nausea, vomiting, and back pain — a classic pancreatitis presentation — and the CT chest incidentally revealed calcified gallstones, making gallstone-related pancreatitis or acute cholecystitis the most actionable biliary diagnoses to pursue. A dedicated liver/gallbladder ultrasound will directly assess for gallbladder inflammation, biliary ductal dilation, or choledocholithiasis that CT chest could not adequately characterize, while also providing better detail on the hepatic lesions flagged as potentially metastatic. This is the appropriate next step to localize the source of the biliary/pancreatic pathology before deciding on further management.

grounding: History: nausea/vomitting subsequently. Later that evening, develop lower back pain
Prior imaging CT Chest: calcified gallstones within the gallbladder
Prior imaging CT Chest: numerous ill-defined hypodense hepatic lesions which measure up to approximately 2.5-cm... concerning for metastatic disease
History: stage III colon CA... recently started FOLFOX last ___ finished on ___
History: constipated, lethargy through the weekend
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has a lipase more than three times the upper limit of normal alongside nausea, vomiting, and back pain — a classic pancreatitis presentation — and the CT chest incidentally revealed calcified gallstones, making gallstone-related pancreatitis or acute cholecystitis the most actionable biliary diagnoses to pursue. A dedicated liver/gallbladder ultrasound will directly assess for gallbladder inflammation, biliary ductal dilation, or choledocholithiasis that CT chest could not adequately characterize, while also providing better detail on the hepatic lesions flagged as potentially metastatic. verification.actual_finding: Multiple hypoechoic hepatic lesions corresponding to CT findings were identified, but there was no cholecystitis, no biliary ductal dilation (CBD 3 mm), and no intrahepatic ductal dilation, despite the presence of multiple gallstones.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected evidence of acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis with CBD dilation, or hepatic lesion characterization, but the ultrasound showed no biliary obstruction or gallbladder inflammation, making biliary pathology an unlikely driver of the elevated lipase, while the hepatic lesions were identified but not further characterized beyond their hypoechoic appearance.
